title: Dental & Medical Group Accounting: Provider- and Location-Level Profitability from QuickBooks Online
excerpt: How dental and medical groups get provider-, location-, and service-line profitability from QuickBooks Online, and how they consolidate multi-entity books.
author: FinBoard Team
coverAlt: Dental & Medical Group Accounting: Provider- and Location-Level Profitability from QuickBooks Online

@graph.headline: Dental & Medical Group Accounting: Provider- and Location-Level Profitability from QuickBooks Online
@graph.description: A practical guide for dental and medical groups on QuickBooks Online: class tracking by provider and service line, location-level P&Ls, production vs. collections revenue recognition, practice management system mapping (OpenDental, Dentrix, Eaglesoft), multi-entity PC/PLLC and DSO consolidation, shared cost allocation, and how FinBoard.ai delivers consolidated, drillable group reporting.

@graph.about: Provider- and location-level profitability reporting and multi-entity consolidation for dental and medical groups using QuickBooks Online

mainEntity.name: How do I track profitability by location for a dental practice in QuickBooks Online?

acceptedAnswer.text: Enable location tracking (QBO Plus or Advanced), assign one Location per physical office, and tag every transaction to its office, including daysheet revenue entries and expenses like rent, payroll, and supplies. Run the P&L by Location report monthly. If offices are separate legal entities with separate QBO files, you also need a consolidation layer such as FinBoard.ai, since QuickBooks Online cannot report across files.

mainEntity.name: Should I record production or collections as revenue in QuickBooks Online?

acceptedAnswer.text: Record gross production as revenue with contractual write-offs in contra-revenue accounts, and carry insurance and patient AR on the balance sheet. Pure cash-basis bookkeeping (deposits = revenue) hides AR problems and misstates the month work was performed. The production-to-collections gap, meaning PPO write-offs plus AR timing, is one of the most important practice metrics, and it only becomes visible when both sides are booked.

mainEntity.name: Can QuickBooks Online consolidate multiple dental practice entities?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Each PC or PLLC has its own QuickBooks Online file, and QBO has no native multi-entity consolidation: no combined statements, no intercompany eliminations, no cross-file reporting. Groups either assemble the consolidated view manually in spreadsheets each month or use a consolidation platform like FinBoard.ai that syncs every entity, maps accounts to one group chart, eliminates intercompany management fees, and produces drillable consolidated statements.

mainEntity.name: How should a dental group structure its chart of accounts?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mirror the revenue lifecycle: gross patient service revenue, contra accounts for PPO write-offs and adjustments, and separate insurance AR and patient AR. Group direct clinical costs (lab fees, supplies) apart from overhead, and split provider compensation from hygiene and admin payroll. Use the identical structure in every entity so location comparison and consolidation work without translation.

content:
Dental & Medical Group Accounting: Provider- and Location-Level Profitability from QuickBooks Online

Ask the owner of a three-location dental group which office made money last month and you will usually get a confident answer. Ask which provider made money, or whether the ortho service line is actually covering its lab fees and chair time, and the confidence evaporates. The data exists. It sits in the practice management system and in three or four separate QuickBooks Online files, but nobody has wired it together into a view that answers the question.

For dental and medical groups running on QuickBooks Online, three things have to work: classes, locations, and a chart of accounts structured so profitability is measurable at the provider, location, and service-line level; a fix for the production-versus-collections trap that distorts almost every practice P&L; and a consolidated group view even though every legal entity lives in its own QBO file.

Why Practice P&Ls Mislead: Production vs. Collections

Dental and medical revenue has a complexity most industries never deal with: the amount you produce is almost never the amount you collect.

Your practice management system, whether OpenDental, Dentrix, Eaglesoft, or an EHR on the medical side, records production: procedures performed, valued at your full fee schedule. But what actually lands in the bank is collections, which arrives weeks or months later, shrunk by three separate forces:

  • PPO write-offs and contractual adjustments. If your fee is $1,200 for a crown and the PPO contracted rate is $850, that $350 is production you will never collect. On a heavy-PPO book, write-offs can run 25 to 40% of gross production.

  • Insurance accounts receivable. Claims are submitted, adjudicated, sometimes denied and resubmitted. Insurance AR routinely lags production by 30 to 60 days, which means a strong production month can look like a weak revenue month in QBO, and vice versa.

  • Patient portions. Co-pays, deductibles, and balances after insurance. Patient AR behaves differently from insurance AR. It collects slower and writes off more often.

Here is the trap: many practices book revenue in QuickBooks Online only when deposits hit the bank. That cash-basis view tells you nothing about the month the work was actually done, hides deteriorating AR, and makes provider and location comparisons meaningless whenever collection timing differs between offices. A location with a great biller looks more profitable than a location with a great dentist.

The fix is to bring the practice management system's daysheet into QBO as a structured journal entry, with production, adjustments, and collections as separate movements, so the P&L reflects work performed and the balance sheet carries real AR. We will cover the exact mapping below.

The Three Profitability Dimensions Every Group Should Track

Well-run dental and medical groups slice profitability three ways. QuickBooks Online can support all three, but you have to be deliberate about which QBO mechanism carries which dimension, because you only get one class field and one location field per transaction line.

DimensionQBO mechanismExample valuesQuestions it answers
ProviderClasses (or sub-classes under location)Dr. Patel, Dr. Nguyen, Hygiene Team A, Visiting Oral SurgeonProvider contribution margin, associate compensation vs. production, hygienist productivity
LocationLocations feature (QBO Plus or Advanced)Downtown, Northside, RiverviewOffice-level P&L, rent vs. revenue, same-store growth, which office to expand
Service lineClasses or Products/Services itemsGeneral dentistry, Hygiene, Cosmetic, Ortho, ImplantsMix shift, marketing ROI by line, whether ortho covers its lab and chair costs

Setting Up Classes and Locations in QuickBooks Online

A few practical rules that save painful re-tagging later:

  • You need QBO Plus or Advanced. Class and location tracking are not available on Simple Start or Essentials. For a multi-provider group this upgrade pays for itself in the first month.

  • Use Locations for physical offices, Classes for your primary analytical dimension. Location is a header-level field, perfect for "which office." Class applies per line, so it can carry either provider or service line. Pick the one that drives more decisions. For most groups that is provider, because compensation, scheduling, and recruiting decisions all hang off provider economics.

  • Use sub-classes if you need two layers. A structure like Downtown:Dr. Patel or Ortho:Dr. Nguyen works, but keep it two levels deep at most. Beyond that, tagging discipline collapses and reports become unreadable.

  • Carry service line on Products/Services items. If provider takes the class field, map service lines through items tied to income accounts (or item categories). Sales by Product/Service reports then give you the mix view without burning your one class dimension.

  • Tag expenses, not just revenue. Lab fees, clinical supplies, and provider compensation should carry the same class as the revenue they support. A provider P&L with untagged costs is just a revenue report wearing a costume.

A Dental Practice Chart of Accounts That Supports This

The chart of accounts should mirror the production-to-collections flow instead of hiding it:

  • Revenue: Patient Service Revenue (gross production), with contra-revenue accounts for PPO/contractual write-offs, other adjustments, and refunds. Net revenue becomes visible as a computed line, and your write-off percentage becomes a trackable KPI instead of an invisible haircut.

  • Receivables: Separate Insurance AR and Patient AR accounts. They age differently, collect differently, and deserve different collection strategies.

  • Direct clinical costs: Lab fees, clinical supplies, and implant/ortho parts as their own cost group. These behave like COGS and scale with production.

  • Compensation: Split provider compensation (associates on collections- or production-based pay), hygiene payroll, and front-office/admin payroll. Clinical payroll flexes with chair usage and provider schedules; admin payroll is mostly fixed. Mixing them into one "Wages" account destroys your ability to model capacity.

  • Facility and occupancy: Rent, utilities, and equipment leases per location, tagged by Location so office-level margin is real.

One non-negotiable for groups: use the same chart of accounts structure in every entity's QBO file. Consolidation, comparison, and benchmarking all get an order of magnitude harder when Downtown books lab fees to "Laboratory Costs" and Northside books them to "Outside Services."

Mapping the Practice Management System into QBO

OpenDental, Dentrix, and Eaglesoft all produce an end-of-day (or end-of-month) report that summarizes production, adjustments, and collections. The clean mapping into QuickBooks Online is a recurring journal entry per location:

  • Production: debit Insurance AR / Patient AR, credit Patient Service Revenue, tagged by provider class.

  • Contractual write-offs: debit the contra-revenue adjustment account, credit AR.

  • Collections: debit Undeposited Funds or the bank account, credit the appropriate AR account, split between insurance payments and patient payments.

Monthly, reconcile the AR balance in QBO against the practice management system's aged AR. If they drift, your revenue recognition has a leak, usually unposted adjustments or payments applied in one system but not the other.

The Multi-Entity Problem: One Group, Many QuickBooks Files

Now the part that breaks spreadsheets. Dental and medical groups almost never operate as one legal entity. State corporate-practice rules typically require each clinical practice to be a professional corporation (PC) or PLLC, often one per state or one per practice, frequently owned by a licensed provider. DSO and MSO structures add a management company that employs admin staff, holds leases, and charges management fees to each practice entity.

Each entity gets its own QuickBooks Online file, and QBO has no native consolidation. There is no built-in way to see the group P&L, compare locations side by side, or eliminate the management fees that the MSO bills and each PC pays. So the group view becomes a month-end ritual: export each file to Excel, align charts of accounts by hand, paste, adjust eliminations, and hope nobody edited a prior period. We have written about this pattern in detail in multi-entity consolidation gaps and how to close them. Healthcare groups are among the worst affected because entity count grows with every acquisition.

The structural tension is that you need both things at once: consolidated visibility for owners, lenders, and DSO partners, and strict entity separation for legal, tax, and licensure reasons. Merging the books is not an option. The answer has to be a reporting layer above the entities, not a restructuring of them.

Allocating Shared Corporate Costs

Once an MSO exists, shared costs pile up in it: central billing and insurance follow-up, marketing, HR, IT, executive compensation. Leaving them unallocated makes every practice look more profitable than it is and makes the MSO look like a money pit. Allocating them badly starts partner arguments.

Practical allocation guidance:

  • Pick a defensible base per cost type. Central billing allocates well on collections; marketing on new-patient counts or location revenue; IT and HR on headcount; occupancy support on chair count or square footage.

  • Document the methodology once and apply it every month. Consistency matters more than precision. A management fee methodology that changes quarterly is a red flag for auditors and for the IRS (management fees between related entities must be defensible as arm's-length).

  • Book the allocation as intercompany management fees, income in the MSO and expense in each PC, and remember these must eliminate in any consolidated view, or group revenue and expenses are both overstated.

What Good Looks Like

Groups that get this right converge on the same monthly package:

  • Consolidated P&L by location, delivered within a few business days of close, with management fees eliminated and every location on the identical account structure.

  • Provider contribution margin: collections attributable to each provider, minus their compensation, lab fees, and clinical supplies, minus an allocated share of chair and facility cost. This is the number that should drive associate contracts and scheduling, not gross production.

  • Service-line mix trends: hygiene vs. restorative vs. cosmetic vs. ortho over time, so marketing spend follows margin instead of vanity volume.

  • Production-to-collections bridge: gross production, write-off %, net collections, and AR days, per location, every month.

  • Same-store comparisons and budget vs. actual per office, so a new acquisition's ramp does not blur the performance of mature locations.

And all of it should be reproducible and traceable, with every consolidated number drillable to the entity transactions behind it. That auditability matters more as groups take on DSO investment or bank debt; see our guide to designing audit-ready controls without slowing the close for how to keep evidence and speed at the same time.

How FinBoard.ai Handles the Consolidation and Reporting Layer

FinBoard.ai is built for exactly this shape of problem: multiple QuickBooks Online entities that must stay legally separate but report as one group.

  • Connect every entity's QBO file, from each PC and PLLC to the MSO, with continuous sync, so the group view is always current instead of month-end-only.

  • Auto-map charts of accounts across entities into a single group structure. When Downtown and Northside name accounts differently, FinBoard's mapping layer reconciles them into one canonical hierarchy without touching the underlying files.

  • Consolidated P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow with intercompany eliminations handled: management fees, intercompany loans, and shared-cost recharges net out of the group statements.

  • Class and location dimensions preserved, so the consolidated report still slices by provider, office, and service line, and every number drills down to the underlying QBO transactions for verification.

  • Google Sheets sync pushes live consolidated reports into the models your CFO, DSO partners, and lenders already use, refreshed automatically instead of rebuilt by hand.

The result: entity separation for the lawyers, one set of numbers for the operators, and no monthly spreadsheet assembly.

Implementation Checklist

  • 1. Standardize the chart of accounts across every entity, including contra-revenue accounts for write-offs and separate insurance/patient AR.

  • 2. Upgrade each QBO file to Plus or Advanced and enable class and location tracking.

  • 3. Assign dimensions: Locations = physical offices; Classes = providers (or provider under location); items = service lines.

  • 4. Build the daysheet journal entry from OpenDental/Dentrix/Eaglesoft: production, adjustments, and collections, posted at least monthly per location.

  • 5. Reconcile PM-system AR to QBO AR monthly, insurance and patient separately.

  • 6. Tag direct costs (lab fees, clinical supplies, provider comp) with the same classes as revenue.

  • 7. Document the management fee / cost allocation methodology and book it identically every month.

  • 8. Stand up the consolidation layer: connect all entities, map accounts, configure eliminations.

  • 9. Define the monthly package: consolidated P&L by location, provider contribution margin, production-to-collections bridge, AR days.

  • 10. Review provider and location margins with operators monthly. Reports that nobody acts on are just decoration.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I track profitability by location for a dental practice in QuickBooks Online?

Enable location tracking (QBO Plus or Advanced), assign one Location per physical office, and tag every transaction to its office, including revenue entries from the daysheet and expenses like rent, payroll, and supplies. Run the P&L by Location report monthly. If your offices are separate legal entities with separate QBO files, you additionally need a consolidation layer such as FinBoard.ai to see them side by side and combined, since QBO cannot report across files.

Should I record production or collections as revenue in QuickBooks Online?

Record gross production as revenue with contractual write-offs in contra-revenue accounts, and carry insurance and patient AR on the balance sheet. Pure cash-basis (deposits = revenue) hides AR problems and misstates the month work was performed. The gap between production and collections, meaning PPO write-offs plus AR timing, is itself one of the most important metrics a practice can watch, and it only becomes visible when both sides are booked.

Can QuickBooks Online consolidate multiple dental practice entities?

No. Each PC or PLLC has its own QBO file, and QuickBooks Online has no native multi-entity consolidation: no combined statements, no intercompany eliminations, no cross-file reporting. Groups either assemble the consolidated view manually in spreadsheets each month or use a consolidation platform like FinBoard.ai that syncs every entity, maps accounts to one group chart, eliminates intercompany management fees, and produces drillable consolidated statements.

How should a dental group structure its chart of accounts?

Mirror the revenue lifecycle: gross patient service revenue, contra accounts for PPO write-offs and adjustments, and separate insurance AR and patient AR. Group direct clinical costs (lab fees, supplies) apart from overhead, and split provider compensation from hygiene and admin payroll since clinical pay flexes with chair usage while admin pay is fixed. Most importantly, use the identical structure in every entity so location comparison and consolidation work without translation.

Ready to see every provider, location, and entity in one set of numbers? Connect your QuickBooks Online files to FinBoard.ai and get consolidated, drillable group reporting without the month-end spreadsheet assembly.
